moments.headline: Merchant 154, Hazare 164*: Delhi run-feast
moments.story: In the first Test against England at Delhi (2–7 November 1951), 40-year-old Vijay Merchant signed off from Test cricket with 154 — still the oldest Indian to score a Test century. Captain Vijay Hazare went past him with an unbeaten 164 as the pair added 211, lifting India to 418 and a proud draw.

moments.headline: Madras 1952: India's first ever Test win
moments.story: On 10 February 1952, after 20 years and 24 Tests of trying, India beat England by an innings and 8 runs at Madras. Vinoo Mankad was unplayable, taking 8/55 and 4/53 for a 12-wicket match haul. Later that year India also claimed their first series win, beating Pakistan 2-1 at home.

moments.headline: Umrigar 223: India's first double century
moments.story: At Hyderabad on 19–24 November 1955, Polly Umrigar batted more than eight hours against New Zealand for 223 — the first double century ever scored by an Indian in Test cricket. His 26-four marathon carried India to 498/4 declared in a high-scoring draw.

moments.headline: Mankad-Roy 413: world-record opening stand
moments.story: On 6–7 January 1956 at Madras, Vinoo Mankad (231) and Pankaj Roy (173) put on 413 for the first wicket against New Zealand — a world record that stood for 52 years. Mankad's 231 was then the highest Test score by an Indian, and India won by an innings and 109 runs.

moments.headline: Gupte 9/102: leg-spin wizardry at Kanpur
moments.story: In the Kanpur Test beginning 12 December 1958, leg-spinner Subhash Gupte took 9/102 against the mighty West Indies — the first nine-wicket innings haul by an Indian. He was denied all ten only when the keeper dropped Lance Gibbs; the spell inspired a young Bishan Bedi listening on the radio.

moments.headline: Patel 9/69: Australia stunned at Kanpur
moments.story: On 24 December 1959, off-spinner Jasu Patel completed the 'miracle at Kanpur' — 9/69 in the first innings and 14 wickets in the match — as India beat Richie Benaud's Australia for the very first time. His 9/69 remained India's best Test innings figures until Anil Kumble's perfect ten, forty years later.

moments.headline: England beaten 2-0: first series win
moments.story: Wins at Calcutta and Madras (sealed 15 January 1962) gave India their first-ever series victory over England, with Chandu Borde, Salim Durani and a 103 from young Tiger Pataudi doing the damage. That March in Barbados, Pataudi, aged just 21 years and 77 days, became the youngest Test captain in history.

moments.headline: Pataudi 203*: one-eyed double century
moments.story: At the Feroz Shah Kotla in February 1964, captain Mansur Ali Khan Pataudi — batting with one good eye after his 1961 car accident — hammered an unbeaten 203 against England. Walking in with India trailing by over 100, he turned the match with the biggest innings of his career.

moments.headline: Pataudi 75 & 85 on one leg
moments.story: In the Melbourne Test beginning 30 December 1967, Tiger Pataudi came in at 25/5 with a torn hamstring and his famously damaged eye — and made 75 and 85, batting virtually on one leg. Australia won, but the innings became Indian cricket's defining tale of courage.

moments.headline: First away conquest: New Zealand beaten 3-1
moments.story: On 20 February 1968 at Dunedin, India won a Test outside India for the first time in 36 years of trying, Erapalli Prasanna's 6/94 setting up a five-wicket win. Pataudi's men went on to take the series 3-1 — India's first overseas series victory.

moments.headline: Debut ton: Viswanath 137 at Kanpur
moments.story: After a first-innings duck on debut, 20-year-old Gundappa Viswanath caressed 137 against Australia at Kanpur, completed on 20 November 1969 — 354 minutes of wristy elegance with 25 fours. India had found the artist who would enchant a generation.

moments.headline: 1971: West Indies AND England conquered
moments.story: India's miracle year. In March–April, debutant Sunil Gavaskar plundered 774 runs as India won at Port of Spain to take their first series in the West Indies. Then on 24 August at The Oval, Chandrasekhar's 6/38 skittled England for 101 and Ajit Wadekar's men sealed a first-ever series win in England.

moments.headline: Eden New Year Test: spinners strike back
moments.story: In the Calcutta Test beginning 30 December 1972, Bishan Bedi (5/63) and Chandrasekhar ripped through England to square the series in front of a roaring Eden Gardens. The 28-run win set up India's famous home-series triumph completed weeks later.

moments.headline: England beaten 2-1: Chandra's 35 wickets
moments.story: India chased down their target at Madras on 17 January 1973 to go 2-1 up, and held on to beat England in the five-Test series. Chandrasekhar's 35 wickets — still an Indian series record then — and Farokh Engineer's 415 runs made it back-to-back series wins over England.

moments.headline: First ODI ever: India take the field
moments.story: On 13 July 1974 at Headingley, Leeds, Ajit Wadekar's India played their first-ever One-Day International, making 265 against England with Brijesh Patel top-scoring on 82. England won by four wickets — but the format that would make India a cricketing superpower had arrived.

moments.headline: First World Cup: East Africa routed
moments.story: India played in the inaugural World Cup in England, and on 11 June 1975 at Headingley recorded their first-ever ODI win, bowling East Africa out for 120 and cruising home by 10 wickets. Sunil Gavaskar (65*) and Farokh Engineer (54*) knocked off the runs without losing a wicket.

moments.headline: 406/4: world-record chase, Port of Spain
moments.story: On 12 April 1976 at Port of Spain, India chased down 403 against the West Indies — then the highest successful fourth-innings chase in Test history. Sunil Gavaskar and Gundappa Viswanath both made centuries, and only two wickets fell to Windies bowlers all day.

moments.headline: Chandra 6/52 twice: first win in Australia
moments.story: In the Melbourne Test starting 30 December 1977, Chandrasekhar took 6/52 in each innings — identical figures, a Test first — and Gavaskar scored a hundred as India crushed Australia by 222 runs. Sealed in the new year, it was India's first Test win on Australian soil.

moments.headline: Enter Kapil Dev: debut at Faisalabad
moments.story: On 16 October 1978 at Faisalabad, a 19-year-old from Haryana named Kapil Dev made his Test debut against Pakistan, startling the batsmen with genuine pace and claiming Sadiq Mohammad with his trademark outswinger. Indian cricket's greatest all-rounder — and its 1983 destiny — had arrived.

moments.headline: Gavaskar 221: nine runs from a miracle
moments.story: Set 438 at The Oval, India finished 429/8 on 4 September 1979 — nine short of the greatest chase ever — after Sunil Gavaskar's monumental 221, a seven-and-a-half-hour masterpiece. The drawn Test is remembered as one of the finest fourth-innings assaults in history.

moments.headline: Viswanath recalls Taylor: honour over victory
moments.story: In the Golden Jubilee Test at Bombay (15–19 February 1980), captain Gundappa Viswanath recalled England's Bob Taylor after a wrongful caught-behind decision. Taylor and Ian Botham (114 and 13 wickets) then took the match away — a defeat India wears as a badge of sportsmanship.

moments.headline: Kapil 5/28: Melbourne miracle levels series
moments.story: Defending just 143 at the MCG on 11 February 1981, an injured Kapil Dev bowled 16 overs unchanged on a strained hamstring and took 5/28 as Australia crumbled for 83. The 59-run win squared the series 1-1 — the first time India had avoided defeat in a series Down Under.

moments.headline: Kapil 89 off 55: gunfire at Lord's
moments.story: With an innings defeat looming at Lord's on 14 June 1982, Kapil Dev launched a savage counterattack — 89 off just 55 balls with 13 fours and three sixes. Wisden wrote that his bat made 'the sound of gunfire'; he was Man of the Match even in defeat.

moments.headline: World champions! West Indies 140 all out
moments.story: On 25 June 1983 at Lord's, Kapil Dev's underdogs defended just 183 to beat the twice-champion West Indies by 43 runs and win the World Cup. Kapil's over-the-shoulder sprinting catch to remove Viv Richards turned the final; Mohinder Amarnath (26 and 3/12) was Man of the Match. Indian cricket changed forever.

moments.headline: Inaugural Asia Cup: India champions in Sharjah
moments.story: India won the first-ever Asia Cup, played at Sharjah from 6–13 April 1984, beating both Sri Lanka and Pakistan in the round-robin. Wicketkeeper Surinder Khanna's match-winning knock against Pakistan sealed it, and Sunil Gavaskar lifted the trophy in the desert.

moments.headline: Champions again: Shastri drives home the Audi
moments.story: On 10 March 1985, India beat Pakistan by 8 wickets at the MCG to win the Benson & Hedges World Championship of Cricket, unbeaten through the tournament. Ravi Shastri, named Champion of Champions, drove his prize Audi 100 around the ground with the whole team piled on board.

moments.headline: Lord's conquered: first Test win there
moments.story: On 10 June 1986, Kapil Dev's India completed a five-wicket victory over England — their first-ever Test win at Lord's. Dilip Vengsarkar stroked an unbeaten 126, his third straight hundred at the home of cricket, and Kapil finished it with a six. India went on to take the series 2-0.

moments.headline: Gavaskar 10,000: cricket's Everest scaled
moments.story: On 7 March 1987 at Ahmedabad, against Pakistan, Sunil Gavaskar became the first batsman in history to reach 10,000 Test runs. The normally unflappable opener sprinted down the pitch in celebration, and the game paused for a 20-minute ovation.

moments.headline: Hirwani 16/136: greatest debut ever
moments.story: Bespectacled 19-year-old leg-spinner Narendra Hirwani took 8/61 and 8/75 on debut as India crushed the West Indies at Madras on 15 January 1988. His match figures of 16/136 remain the best ever by a Test debutant.

moments.headline: A boy named Sachin debuts in Karachi
moments.story: On 15 November 1989, a 16-year-old Sachin Tendulkar walked out at Karachi to face Imran Khan, Wasim Akram and fellow debutant Waqar Younis. He made just 15, but the most storied career in cricket — 24 years and 100 international hundreds — had begun.

moments.headline: Tendulkar 119*: first of a hundred
moments.story: On 14 August 1990 at Old Trafford, 17-year-old Sachin Tendulkar scored his maiden international century — an unbeaten 119 that saved the Test against England. It was the first of his record 100 international hundreds.

moments.headline: Eden Gardens welcomes South Africa back
moments.story: On 10 November 1991, a packed Eden Gardens hosted South Africa's first international match in 21 years, ending their apartheid-era isolation. India won the historic ODI by three wickets; South African captain Clive Rice said he felt like Neil Armstrong standing on the moon.

moments.headline: Teen Tendulkar tames Perth: 114
moments.story: On 3 February 1992, on the fastest, bounciest pitch in the world at the WACA, 18-year-old Sachin Tendulkar carved 114 off 161 balls against Merv Hughes and Craig McDermott. Tendulkar later called it the innings that convinced him he could take on the world.

moments.headline: Hero Cup: Kumble 6/12 flattens Windies
moments.story: On 27 November 1993, Anil Kumble's 6/12 demolished the West Indies for 123 as India won the Hero Cup final by 102 runs at Eden Gardens. Days earlier, Sachin Tendulkar had bowled the last over of the semi-final against South Africa, defending six runs and conceding just three.

moments.headline: Kapil 432: world wicket record
moments.story: At 10.34am on 8 February 1994 in Ahmedabad, Kapil Dev had Sri Lanka's Hashan Tillakaratne caught at short leg — Test wicket number 432, passing Sir Richard Hadlee as the leading wicket-taker in history. The city released 432 balloons in celebration.

moments.headline: Asia Cup: Azhar 90* strolls home, Sharjah
moments.story: On 14 April 1995 at Sharjah, India won the Asia Cup final by eight wickets, cruising past Sri Lanka's 230 with 49 balls to spare. Mohammad Azharuddin's unbeaten 90 and a 175-run stand with Navjot Sidhu made it India's fourth Asia Cup title.

moments.headline: Bangalore QF: Pakistan beaten, Prasad roars
moments.story: In the home World Cup's fever-pitch quarter-final at Bangalore on 9 March 1996, Navjot Sidhu's 93 and Ajay Jadeja's 45-off-25 blitz set Pakistan 288. Venkatesh Prasad uprooted Aamer Sohail's off stump right after the batsman's taunt — the send-off of a generation — as India won by 39 runs.

moments.headline: Toronto: Ganguly torments Pakistan, 4-1
moments.story: In the Sahara Cup at Toronto, 13–21 September 1997, Sourav Ganguly produced one of ODI cricket's great all-round streaks — 222 runs, 15 wickets including 5/16, and four straight Man of the Match awards — as India beat Pakistan 4-1. The Prince of Calcutta had announced himself.

moments.headline: Desert Storm: Sachin 143 in Sharjah
moments.story: On 22 April 1998 at Sharjah, with a literal sandstorm halting play, Sachin Tendulkar blew Australia away with 143 off 131 balls to drag India into the Coca-Cola Cup final. Two nights later, on his 25th birthday, he hit 134 more to win the trophy — the twin knocks forever known as Desert Storm.

moments.headline: Kumble 10/74: all ten wickets
moments.story: On 7 February 1999 at the Feroz Shah Kotla, Anil Kumble took all ten Pakistan wickets in an innings — 10/74 — as India won by 212 runs. Only Jim Laker before him had done it in Test history; teammate Javagal Srinath famously bowled wide of off stump so as not to spoil the perfect ten.

moments.headline: Nairobi: Ganguly 117, new India rises
moments.story: In the ICC KnockOut final at Nairobi on 15 October 2000, captain Sourav Ganguly struck 117 — his second hundred of the tournament — though Chris Cairns' 102* stole the trophy for New Zealand. In a year scarred by the match-fixing scandal, the fearless run of Ganguly's young side, with Yuvraj Singh and Zaheer Khan emerging, rebuilt a nation's faith.

moments.headline: Eden epic: Laxman 281, Australia broken
moments.story: Following on at Eden Gardens, VVS Laxman (281) and Rahul Dravid (180) batted a full day together, adding 376, before Harbhajan Singh — who had already taken India's first Test hat-trick — finished Australia off on 15 March 2001. The 171-run win ended Australia's record 16-Test streak and is routinely voted the greatest Test of the modern era.

moments.headline: NatWest final: 326 chased, shirt waved
moments.story: On 13 July 2002 at Lord's, India hunted down England's 326 — then the highest successful final chase — through Yuvraj Singh (69) and Mohammad Kaif's cool 87*. Captain Sourav Ganguly whirled his shirt from the Lord's balcony, an image seared into Indian sporting memory.

moments.headline: World Cup final run: Sachin's 673
moments.story: Ganguly's India won eight matches in a row to reach the World Cup final on 23 March 2003, where Ricky Ponting's Australia proved too strong. The tournament still belonged to Sachin Tendulkar — a record 673 runs and Player of the Tournament, including his iconic upper-cut six off Shoaib Akhtar in the win over Pakistan at Centurion on 1 March.

moments.headline: Sehwag 309: the Sultan of Multan
moments.story: On 29 March 2004 at Multan, Virender Sehwag became the first Indian to score a Test triple century — 309 off 386 balls, reaching the landmark with a six. India won by an innings and went on to take the series 2-1, their first-ever Test series victory in Pakistan.

moments.headline: Sachin's 35th: Gavaskar's record falls
moments.story: On 10 December 2005 at the Feroz Shah Kotla, Sachin Tendulkar scored 109 against Sri Lanka — his 35th Test century, passing Sunil Gavaskar's world record of 34. India won by 188 runs as one Mumbai master overtook another.

moments.headline: Caribbean conquered after 35 years
moments.story: On 2 July 2006 at Sabina Park, Kingston, Rahul Dravid's gritty 81 and 68 on a spiteful pitch carried India to a 49-run win and a 1-0 series victory — their first in the West Indies since the heroes of 1971. Dravid was Man of the Match and Man of the Series.

moments.headline: T20 world champions: Pakistan pipped by five
moments.story: On 24 September 2007 in Johannesburg, MS Dhoni's young India beat Pakistan by five runs in the first-ever T20 World Cup final. With Misbah-ul-Haq threatening the impossible, Dhoni tossed the last over to rookie Joginder Sharma — and the scooped catch settled into Sreesanth's hands at short fine leg.

moments.headline: Perth stormed; the IPL is born
moments.story: On 19 January 2008, days after the acrimonious Sydney Test, Anil Kumble's India stunned Australia by 72 runs at the WACA — the first Asian team to win a Test at Perth, ending the hosts' 16-match winning streak. On 18 April, Brendon McCullum's 158* lit up the first-ever IPL match in Bangalore, and cricket entered a new age.

moments.headline: India No.1 Test team: Sehwag 293
moments.story: On 6 December 2009, an innings win over Sri Lanka at Mumbai's Brabourne Stadium took India to No.1 in the ICC Test rankings for the first time. Virender Sehwag's 293 — 284 of them in a single day — powered India to 726/9 declared.

moments.headline: Sachin 200*: ODI's first double century
moments.story: On 24 February 2010 at Gwalior, Sachin Tendulkar made an unbeaten 200 off 147 balls against South Africa — the first double century in men's ODI history. The 37-year-old broke a barrier the format had guarded for four decades.

moments.headline: World champions! Dhoni finishes in style
moments.story: On 2 April 2011 at Mumbai's Wankhede Stadium, MS Dhoni promoted himself, made 91*, and launched Nuwan Kulasekara over long-on to win the World Cup — India's first in 28 years. Gautam Gambhir's 97 anchored the chase of 275, and a tearful Sachin Tendulkar was carried around his home ground on his teammates' shoulders.
